judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is stronger overall — it commits to explicit, unhedged fixes for every issue (notably fail-closed on Redis error), whereas Report 1 hedges on the rate-limiter fail-open behavior instead of recommending denial.

report_1.evidence: BadRequestError" — caught, same fallover. Not appropriate. ... ContentPolicyError" — caught, same fallover. Not appropriate. ... AuthError" — caught, same fallover. Not appropriate. ... The fix is to catch only the transient/vendor-availability classes (RateLimitError, RequestTimeoutError, ServerError) for fallover, and let BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, and AuthError propagate

report_2.evidence: BadRequestError: inappropriate to fail over. ... ContentPolicyError: inappropriate and the most serious problem. ... AuthError: inappropriate to fail over. ... Concrete fix: replace the bare except Exception with an explicit denylist function ... except NON_RETRYABLE: raise

report_1.evidence: this is the classic "fail open on infra failure" tradeoff — it prioritizes availability ... whether fail-open vs fail-closed is "right" depends on business priority, but the silence is a real gap either way.

report_2.evidence: Path A is the main defect: this gates safety-relevant behavior on an environment-name string comparison. ... Path C is also a defect: on any redis error ... the limiter fails open ... Concrete fix: invert both defaults. Fail closed unless an explicit dev-only flag is set, and fail closed on RedisError: ... except RedisError: return False

report_1.evidence: It logs the entire document body (input_text) verbatim ... The fix: log the pseudonymized id (reuse the key already computed in batch_score.py, or call pseudonymize(record.applicant_id) here), drop input_text entirely (or log only a hash/length/non-content fingerprint), and log an explicit allow-list of non-sensitive metadata fields rather than the whole dict.

report_2.evidence: input_text is logged verbatim — this is the full sensitive intake document content, logged in the clear into application logs. ... Concrete fix: log the pseudonymized id ..., drop input_text entirely or store only a hash/length, and replace record.metadata with an explicit allowlisted subset

report_1.evidence: Fix: fail loudly rather than defaulting — e.g., salt = os.environ["PII_SALT"] (raising KeyError if unset) or an explicit check that raises a clear configuration error, so a missing salt blocks startup/processing instead of silently downgrading the pseudonymization guarantee.

report_2.evidence: This is a silent fail-open on a missing secret ... Concrete fix: fail hard instead of defaulting: ... salt = os.environ["PII_SALT"] ... if not salt: raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT is not configured; refusing to pseudonymize without a salt")
